data.title: Lamar targets $150M+ M&A activity in 2025 with focus on digital billboard growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lamar Advertising Company (LAMR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean Reilly highlighted that Lamar achieved its 16th consecutive quarter of acquisition-adjusted revenue growth, increasing by 1.1%. Key revenue contributions came from local and programmatic sales, though national revenues showed a slight decline year-over-year. Programmatic revenue grew by nearly 30%, adding $2 million.
  • Reilly shared that digital billboard revenue increased by 4% and represented approximately 30% of total billboard revenue. The company is targeting over 350 new digital billboard deployments in 2025.
  • Lamar completed 10 acquisitions in Q1 for $22 million and projects more than $150 million in M&A activity for the year, exceeding initial guidance.
  • The company repurchased $150 million in shares at an average price of $108, with plans to seek Board approval to expand the repurchase program to $250 million.
  • CFO Jay Johnson reported that adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) totaled $164.3 million, a 3.8% increase year-over-year. AFFO per share grew to $1.60.
  • Johnson noted Lamar's leverage stands at 2.85x net debt to EBITDA, with healthy interest coverage of 6.6x adjusted EBITDA to cash interest.

Outlook

  • Full-year AFFO guidance was affirmed at $8.13 to $8.28 per share. Total leverage is expected to remain at or below 3.0x net debt to EBITDA for 2025.
  • Management projected total capital expenditures of $195 million, including $60 million in maintenance CapEx.
  • CEO Reilly stated that 75% of the company’s 3% organic revenue growth target for 2025 is already booked.
  • Expenses are anticipated to grow approximately 3% for the full year.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 was $210.2 million, with a margin of 41.6%, slightly down from Q1 2024. Consolidated expenses increased by 2.6%, slightly below expectations.
  • Local and regional sales accounted for 82% of billboard revenue, marking continued resilience in the core local advertising business.
  • Programmatic revenue contributed significantly to growth, with a year-over-year increase of $2 million and a nearly 30% growth rate.
  • Digital billboard revenue increased by 4%, supported by a solid performance in digital conversions and existing assets.

Q&A

  • Cameron McVeigh, Morgan Stanley: Questioned national revenue softness and its impact on meeting growth goals. CEO Reilly explained that national revenue softness is compensated by programmatic growth and stable local sales, adding that national ad spend trends are improving.
  • Jason Bazinet, Citi: Asked about signs of economic slowdown and early indicators. Reilly stated that digital sales performance is monitored as an early sign, and current trends show no indications of slowdown.
  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan: Inquired about the M&A pipeline. Reilly highlighted expectations to exceed $200 million in acquisitions for 2025, with significant contributions to revenue growth expected.
  • Daniel Osley, Wells Fargo: Asked about strategies to counter national revenue weakness and digital conversion plans. Reilly confirmed a focus on achieving over 350 digital deployments in 2025.
  • Jonnathan Navarrete, TD Cowen: Queried about AFFO guidance after share repurchases. CFO Johnson clarified that late-quarter repurchases were conservatively excluded from guidance metrics.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about national revenue softness and expense growth, maintaining a slightly skeptical tone. Questions focused on mitigating risks and achieving growth targets amidst economic uncertainty.
  • Management, while addressing analyst concerns, maintained a confident tone, emphasizing the resilience of local advertising and the strategic benefits of programmatic and digital billboard growth. Reilly used phrases such as "steady as she goes" and highlighted solid bookings to reinforce confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similarly optimistic outlook but faced more direct questions regarding national revenue trends and expense management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2025 AFFO per share grew by 3.9% to $1.60, compared to $1.54 in Q4 2024.
  • National revenue softness persisted, though programmatic and digital revenue growth provided a counterbalance.
  • M&A activity significantly ramped up in Q1 2025, with $22 million in deals closed and a projected annual spend exceeding $150 million, compared to $45 million in total acquisitions for 2024.
  • Management reiterated a 3% expense growth target and consistent leverage metrics, reflecting stable financial management.

Risks and Concerns

  • National revenue weakness remains a challenge, though offset by programmatic growth. Management is closely monitoring shifts in ad spend trends.
  • Analysts raised concerns about expense increases driven by health insurance costs and one-time items.
  • Regulatory hurdles for digital billboard deployments and potential delays in M&A integration are ongoing risks.

Final Takeaway

Lamar Advertising delivered stable Q1 2025 results, with resilient local and programmatic revenue growth countering national softness. The company remains on track to exceed its $150 million M&A target for the year, supported by strong leverage and liquidity. AFFO guidance was affirmed, reflecting confidence in achieving organic growth and digital expansion goals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Carlyle group targets 2025 growth with $453B AUM and $84B dry powder
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Carlyle Group (CG) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Harvey Schwartz, CEO, highlighted record fee-related earnings of $311 million, a 17% year-over-year increase, and a record FRE margin of 48%. Assets under management reached an all-time high of $453 billion. Distributable earnings for the quarter were $455 million, the highest in several years.
  • Schwartz emphasized the firm's $84 billion in dry powder and its readiness to capitalize on emerging opportunities, despite macroeconomic uncertainties.
  • Carlyle AlpInvest achieved record FRE, nearly doubling from the prior year, with $89 billion in AUM. The latest portfolio finance fund closed at over $4 billion, more than triple the size of its predecessor.
  • Global Credit achieved over $100 million in quarterly fee-related earnings for the first time, driven by private credit demand and European lending opportunities. Evergreen private credit deployment increased by 150% year-over-year.
  • Fortitude announced over $8 billion in reinsurance transactions, including its sixth transaction in Japan, reflecting Carlyle's strong presence in the market.
  • John Redett, CFO, reported $14 billion in first-quarter inflows, contributing to $50 billion over the last year. Fee-related earnings increased 17% year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Management maintained confidence in achieving 2025 financial targets, supported by growth in Carlyle AlpInvest, Global Credit, and insurance. Schwartz noted, "Our investment horizon and our capital base are long term."
  • Expectations for management fees to increase in Q2 2025 with the activation of fees on the latest U.S. real estate fund.
  • Schwartz discussed strategic initiatives including the launch of a new wealth platform towards the end of 2025 to diversify fundraising streams.

Financial Results

  • Fee-related earnings reached $311 million, marking a 17% increase from Q1 2024. The FRE margin stood at 48%.
  • Global Credit experienced a 28% year-over-year revenue increase, reaching $232 million, supported by capital market fees and strong inflows of $7.5 billion.
  • Carlyle AlpInvest contributed $66 million in FRE, driven by 40% growth in management fees and a record FRE margin of 58%.
  • Realizations in U.S. Buyout strategies amounted to $8 billion over the past year, with significant exits including the IPO of Hexaware Technologies in India and the sales of StandardAero shares and power assets.

Q&A

  • Ben Budish, Barclays, inquired about the impact of trade policy and tariffs on investment. Schwartz noted cautious optimism, emphasizing the firm's ability to deploy capital effectively in dynamic environments.
  • Alex Blostein, Goldman Sachs, asked about the trajectory of the Private Equity franchise. Schwartz highlighted the solid returns and realization activity, including successful IPOs and asset sales, while confirming no major adjustments to the fundraising timeline for CP IX.
  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research, sought clarification on insurance fund flows and their alignment with fundraising targets. Redett confirmed that Fortitude’s flows are included in the $40 billion guidance for 2025.
  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank, questioned expenses and FRE margins. Redett described the 48% FRE margin as sustainable but emphasized ongoing investments in growth areas such as wealth and credit.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the firm's strategic initiatives and strong performance but raised concerns about the macroeconomic environment and potential headwinds in private equity.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly regarding long-term growth and opportunities in AlpInvest, credit, and insurance. Schwartz stressed Carlyle's readiness to mobilize capital and adapt to market conditions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone showed heightened optimism, supported by record metrics and significant achievements in key segments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fee-related earnings increased to $311 million in Q1 2025 from $287 million in Q4 2024, with a higher FRE margin of 48%.
  • Assets under management grew to $453 billion, compared to $423 billion in the previous quarter.
  • Realizations in U.S. Buyout strategies saw continued acceleration, with notable exits including prominent IPOs and asset sales.
  • Management emphasized increased fundraising momentum and strategic initiatives, contrasting with a relatively cautious outlook in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainties, particularly trade policies and their potential second-order effects.
  • Analysts highlighted concerns about elongated sales cycles in private equity and potential stress in endowment allocations to private markets.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include leveraging dry powder, diversifying fundraising streams, and capitalizing on the firm's global platform.

Final Takeaway

Carlyle achieved record results in Q1 2025, driven by strong performance in AlpInvest, Global Credit, and insurance. With $453 billion in AUM and $84 billion in dry powder, the firm remains well-positioned to capitalize on emerging opportunities. Management expressed confidence in meeting 2025 targets, supported by strategic initiatives in wealth, credit, and solutions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Groupon stock soars to three-year high after billings forecast hike, results topper
data.description:
Groupon (NASDAQ:GRPN) shares surged to a three-year high on Thursday after the e-commerce company reported a first-quarter profit and revenue beat, upped its forecast for full-year billings, and reaffirmed its outlook for core metrics.

GRPN stock rose as much as 34.6% to a high of $22.85 on the Nasdaq.

For 2025, the company has raised billings guidance to 3%-5% from 2%-4%. It continues to expect revenue between $493M and $500M (midpoint $496.5M, estimate $496.2M), adjusted EBITDA around $70M to $75M (midpoint $72.5M, estimate $72.4M), and free cash flow of at least $41M.

In North America, Q1 local billings rose 11%, but local revenue remained relatively flat as a result of higher redemption rates as well as lower deal margins, the company said. 

In its international business, excluding the impact of the Italy market exit, local billings were up 5%, and local revenue grew 4%.

"With North America local billings accelerating to double-digit growth and our local marketplace strategy showing green shoots across geographies and verticals, we are building momentum and expect to continue to accelerate our growth. We're focused on delivering sustainable growth by creating exceptional value for both consumers and merchants," CEO Dusan Senkypl said in a statement.

Active customers were 15.5M as of March 31, 2025, up 0.4% sequentially but down 4% from last year. Groupon ended the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $226.8M.

Net income for the quarter was $7.2M, compared with a loss of $12.3M for the same period last year. The prior year period included higher restructuring charges and an interest expense of over $12M.

On a per-share basis, the company earned 17 cents, while analysts on average were expecting the company to lose 14 cents apiece.

Revenue fell 4.8% to $117.2M but beat the average analyst expectations of $115.8M.

data.title: SA analyst downgrades: BTI, HSY, SRPT, HIMS, S
data.description:
Here are the latest rating downgrades by Seeking Alpha analysts:

British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI). Hold to sell by Michael B. Howard. "With management somewhat discredited by their unmet forecasts, new category product revenue now under 10% and valuations corrected, there is no further outsized opportunity."

Hershey (NYSE:HSY). Buy to sell by Michael Duke. "Despite strong brands and management, the current stock price reflects overly optimistic expectations for a return to normalcy, prompting my exit. Tariffs could significantly impact Hershey's (NYSE:HSY) earnings as outlined by management, adding to existing cost pressures and making the stock less attractive."

Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT). Buy to hold by IB's Thoughts. "We see significant risks ahead for Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT), including the unresolved Elevidys hold and safety perception, uneven uptake, sharply higher expenses burning down cash, and a more cautious FDA."

Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT). Buy to hold by Terry Chrisomalis. "SRPT (SRPT) rating changed from Strong Buy to Hold due to uncertainty around sales of DMD drug Elevidys and patient death investigation still underway."

Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS). Buy to hold by Deep Value Investing. "Overall, I rate HIMS (NYSE:HIMS) stock as a Hold, as we could see a pullback to low $40s in the next few days. That said, the stock could resume its long-term upward trend following the pullback."

SentinelOne (NYSE:S). Buy to hold by Uttam Dey. "SentinelOne (NYSE:S) faces significant challenges, including decelerating ARR growth, falling retention rates, and political issues, leading to a downgrade in its investment rating…I recommend staying on the sidelines with SentinelOne (S) until the company overcomes its internal and external pressures."

USA Compression (NYSE:USAC). Buy to hold by Grassroots Trading. "I think USAC (NYSE:USAC) is still a reliable income play, but with horsepower expansion hitting limits and financial leverage creeping higher, I’m not recommending this as an entry for new investors, nor betting on substantial near-term upside."

data.title: Gladstone Commercial plans $70M in acquisitions amid industrial focus shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gladstone Commercial Corporation (GOOD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Gladstone highlighted the acquisition of two industrial facilities for $73 million, emphasizing the company's focus on increasing its portfolio concentration in industrial assets to 70% in the near term. He noted continued efforts to grow the real estate portfolio while maintaining strong underwriting standards.
  • President Buzz Cooper reported the company collected 100% of cash-based rents during Q1 2025, maintained portfolio occupancy at 98.4%, and increased industrial concentration to 65% of annualized straight-line rent. He mentioned that several industrial acquisitions totaling $70 million are under contract for Q2 2025.
  • CFO Gary Gerson detailed financial performance, stating that funds from operations (FFO) and core FFO per share were $0.34 for Q1 2025, consistent with the previous year. He also outlined a manageable debt maturity profile for 2025, with $3.1 million in remaining maturities.

Outlook

  • Management expressed confidence in the company's strategy to focus on acquiring high-quality industrial assets that are mission-critical to tenants. They highlighted opportunities arising from re-shoring and on-shoring trends.
  • Plans to dispose of non-core assets, including office properties, were mentioned as part of the strategy to improve portfolio quality and align with industrial focus.
  • The company anticipates closing $70 million in industrial acquisitions during Q2 2025, supported by $99 million in liquidity from cash and line-of-credit availability.

Financial Results

  • Total operating revenues for Q1 2025 were $37.5 million, compared to $35.7 million in Q1 2024, attributed to higher recovery revenues and rental rates, partially offset by lower variable lease payments from prior property sales.
  • Operating expenses increased to $23.9 million from $23.3 million in Q1 2024, reflecting inflationary pressures and higher incentive fees.
  • Net assets grew from $1.09 billion to $1.16 billion during Q1 2025 due to new acquisitions. The common stock dividend was maintained at $0.30 per share per quarter.

Q&A

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about the acquisition pipeline and funding sources. Buzz Cooper responded that $70 million in industrial acquisitions is under contract for Q2 2025, supported by current liquidity and potential financing options.
  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital: Inquired about acquisition volume and lease expiration management. Buzz Cooper indicated a goal of reaching $100 million in acquisitions for 2025 and detailed proactive lease management efforts to address 2026 and 2027 expirations.
  • John Massocca, B. Riley: Questioned the impact of tariffs and industrial trends. Buzz Cooper reported no immediate tenant concerns but highlighted potential opportunities for industrial growth driven by re-shoring.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the company's ability to execute its industrial acquisition strategy and manage lease expirations effectively, reflecting cautious optimism.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing their disciplined approach and strong liquidity position. Phrases such as "we remain confident" and "well-positioned" reinforced this sentiment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with a continued emphasis on increasing industrial asset concentration to 70%.
  • Management's tone reflected sustained confidence, comparable to the previous quarter, with an ongoing focus on disciplined acquisitions and portfolio improvement.
  • Analyst questions shifted slightly towards acquisition execution and funding strategies, reflecting growing interest in the company's industrial focus.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the challenges posed by higher interest rates and inflation, which have increased operating expenses.
  • Analysts raised concerns about lease expirations in 2026 and 2027, to which management responded with proactive lease negotiation efforts.
  • Potential impacts from tariffs were acknowledged but deemed manageable, with opportunities for industrial growth cited as a mitigating factor.

Final Takeaway

Gladstone Commercial Corporation continues to demonstrate a clear strategic focus on industrial assets, supported by disciplined acquisitions and proactive portfolio management. With $70 million in acquisitions under contract for Q2 2025 and a strong liquidity position, the company aims to achieve its target of 70% industrial concentration, positioning itself well for sustained growth amid macroeconomic challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow rise as U.S. announced new trade deal with UK
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages pushed higher on Thursday as President Donald Trump announced a new a trade deal with the United Kingdom.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was +1.3%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.7%, and the Dow (DJI) was +1.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 6 basis points to 4.33%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 7 basis points to 3.86%.

"We have reached a breakthrough trade deal with the United Kingdom," President Donald Trump said at a press conference on Thursday.

The agreement will provide increased access for billions of dollars of U.S. products, including agricultural products such as beef, ethanol, and "virtually all" of the products. It will also eliminate numerous non-tariff barriers and provided access to American chemicals, machinery, and other products.

“Given that full trade deals take years to negotiate, this will likely be a framework, and it will be interesting to see whether the 10% baseline tariff stays, as that will provide an important template for negotiations with other countries and a good guide to the long-term tariff strategy of the U.S.,” Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

Markets only really care how far and how fast Trump retreats from tariffs, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Initial jobless claims fell more than expected in the past week, while productivity slipped more in Q1 than consensus and labor costs climbed more.

U.S. stocks on Wednesday ended higher, in part due to a sharp upward move in the final half hour of trading. It was triggered by a media report that said Trump was planning to rescind global chip curbs issued during former leader Joe Biden's administration.

The Fed kept its benchmark rate at 4.25%-4.50% on Wednesday for the third straight meeting, as widely expected, even as it sees the risks of inflation and unemployment rising.

Equities saw a muted response to the Fed decision, but the S&P 500 managed to post a gain by the close thanks to a late rally following a report that the Trump administration is planning to rescind Biden-era AI chip curbs as part of a broader move to revise semiconductor trade restrictions, Reid added.

Furthermore, the Fed balance sheet will land later in the day.

data.title: Dutch Bros rallies as analysts say the growth story is still very much in play
data.description:
Dutch Bros (NYSE:BROS) rallied in late morning trading on Thursday after analysts weighed in favorably on the restaurant company's Q1 earnings update.

TD Cowen analyst Andrew Charles called Dutch Bros (NYSE:BROS) one of the few positive revision stories in the restaurant sector. The bullish view on the stock is that near-term traffic-driven execution will persist due to the early-to-middle innings mobile rollout, an expanded food test anticipated to roll out systemwide in 2026, and success with the company's advertising, beverage innovation, and loyalty initiatives.

UBS Dennis Geiger and his team continue to view Dutch Bros (BROS) as well positioned for sales momentum and market share gains. It was noted that a compelling multi-year catalyst path supports potential earnings upside. Geiger said that in the current environment, the firm believes solid positive traffic trends, strong new store productivity, and +20% EBITDA growth should enable further upside for shares.

Morgan Stanley kept an Overweight rating and $80 price target on BROS in place. "This should be one of the cleaner prints this earnings season, and a solid one overall, with comps already known, but revenue better driven by new stores, which flowed through the P&L and some cost lines were more favorable than expected," highlighted analyst Brian Harbour.

Shares of Dutch Bros (BROS) were up 8.8% at 11:38 a.m. to $64.46 vs. the 52-week trading range of $26.96 to $86.88.
